data.title: McGrath projects 2026 revenue of $945M-$995M while keeping adjusted EBITDA outlook of $360M-$378M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: McGrath RentCorp (MGRC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Total company revenues increased 2% and adjusted EBITDA decreased 1% compared to the prior year first quarter." (President, CEO & Director Philip Hawkins)
  • "We delivered rental revenue growth in each of our businesses despite some challenging market conditions." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "At Mobile Modular, rental revenues grew 4%." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "Our commercial market segments were the primary drivers of our growth." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Our Architecture Billings Index, or ABI, and other macro indicators of construction-related demand remained subdued." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "Despite this, our quote and booking levels were higher than a year ago with our geographic expansion efforts and additional sales coverage contributing to these positive trends." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "At TRS, rental revenues continued their recent growth trajectory and were up 13%." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "Demand continued to be strong across the broad spectrum of our equipment, and we benefited from project supporting build-out of new data centers." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "We also worked on a small modular acquisition, which we closed in early April." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "In addition, we completed share repurchases during the quarter." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Total revenues increased 2% to $199 million, and adjusted EBITDA decreased 1% to $74 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Keith E. Pratt)

Outlook

  • "For the full year, our outlook remains unchanged, and we expect total revenue between $945 million and $995 million, adjusted EBITDA between $360 million and $378 million, and gross rental equipment capital expenditures between $180 million and $200 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "Recent developments in the Middle East had no material impact in the first quarter." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "This could change as the year progresses and may increase uncertainty or result in customers delaying projects." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Additionally, higher energy prices for an extended period may start to impact operating costs." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)

Financial Results

  • "Total revenues increased 2% to $199 million, and adjusted EBITDA decreased 1% to $74 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "Inventory center costs increased by $3.2 million as we prepared equipment to support higher shipment levels." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt) "This expense compressed rental margins to 56%, down from 60% a year ago." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "TRS had a strong quarter. with total revenues up 11% to $39 million and adjusted EBITDA up 16% to $21 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt) "Rental margins improved to 45% from 40% a year ago." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "Enviroplex total sales revenue decreased 51% to $3.7 million, and adjusted EBITDA declined to a loss of $1.1 million from a profit of $0.4 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "Net cash provided by operating activities was $42 million compared to $54 million in the prior year." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt) "Rental equipment purchases were $45 million compared to $12 million in the prior year." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "At quarter end, we had net borrowings of $546 million, and the ratio of funded debt to the last 12 months actual adjusted EBITDA was 1.51 to 1." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)

Q&A

  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked which modular end markets are the largest contributors and whether education demand is changing; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said the work "would fall into what you would call the mega project category" and added that "our first quarter education bookings were roughly flat year-over-year."
  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about post-quarter caution tied to the Middle East and where higher energy costs would hit; President, CEO & Director Hawkins said "We haven't seen any material impact" and added "the place that you'd probably see that occur first if this is extended, would be in the fuel cost area" while noting "the majority of those costs were able to pass through the customers"; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt added "It's just way too early to get any read."
  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked why bookings improved while utilization declined; President, CEO & Director Hawkins said "there's some period of time it can be months before those project sites are ready" and Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "we're still getting returns that are higher than the ships."
  • Scott Schneeberger, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., Research Division: asked why MGRC resumed buybacks and whether they continue; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "share repurchases are something we review on a regular basis" and cited "over 1.8 million shares available and authorized for repurchase" under the plan.
  • Scott Schneeberger, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., Research Division: asked about April trends and seasonality; President, CEO & Director Hawkins said April activity was "consistent with what we experienced in Q1" with "solid bookings in Mobile Modular, still kind of flattish in portable storage and continued strength of TRS."
  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked about Mobile Modular price vs. volume; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "we're getting 7% more revenue per unit" and "we had roughly 3% fewer units on rent."
  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group, LLC: asked whether TRS CapEx implies fleet expansion; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "we are more than happy now to add capital" and added "one of the reasons we increased the gross CapEx guide for this year, is, in fact, we saw the probability that this good opportunity at TRS would continue"; President, CEO & Director Hawkins said "I'd pay more attention...to the actual dollars of CapEx and fleet size than units" due to cost variability.
  • Marc Riddick, Sidoti & Company, LLC: asked about competitive pressure in portable storage deliveries/pickups; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "it's harder to cover your costs now than it was 2 or 3 years ago" and added "for us, getting to breakeven would be great."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "Can you kind of talk about how that reconciles internal strength versus weaker indicators?" (John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division) and "Are you seeing green shoots" (Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.) reflected analyst focus on demand durability, utilization pressure, and competitive intensity; Scott Schneeberger’s tone was more upbeat, calling out, "Real nice quarter" and probing capital returns.
  • "We haven't seen any material impact" (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) and "It's just way too early to get any read" (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt) reflected a watchful tone on macro/geopolitical risks, while Hawkins also said "I'm confident we have the right team and discipline in place to drive shareholder value in the years ahead."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s prepared emphasis on leadership transition and 2026 setup, the current call’s tone centered more on execution progress, buybacks, and TRS/data center-driven demand; analyst questions similarly shifted from succession/initial guide framing to utilization, cost pressure, and buyback intent.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1 2026, management reiterated: "For the full year, our outlook remains unchanged" with the same 2026 ranges previously issued. (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • The biggest narrative shift from Q4 2025 to Q1 2026 was leadership: Q4 noted, "It will be my final earnings call as CEO" and "Phil Hawkins is the best leader to succeed me" (President, CEO & Director Joseph Hanna), while Q1 was Hawkins’ first call as CEO.
  • Capital return emphasis increased in Q1, with management stating "we completed share repurchases during the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) and Q&A focusing on the decision; in Q4, buybacks were not a featured topic.
  • TRS momentum remained a consistent theme across both calls, with Q1 reiterating project support for "build-out of new data centers" (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) and Q4 describing "nice demand from data centers" in communications fleet. (President, CEO & Director Hanna)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Our Architecture Billings Index, or ABI, and other macro indicators of construction-related demand remained subdued." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Higher equipment preparation expenses" and "higher costs compressed profitability" were described as pressures in Mobile Modular and portable storage. (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Recent developments in the Middle East had no material impact in the first quarter" but "may increase uncertainty or result in customers delaying projects." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • In portable storage, management highlighted that delivery and pickup economics remain pressured, with Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt saying "we've seen some of the smaller players much more willing to sharpen their pencil" on pricing.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a steady start with "rental revenue growth in each of our divisions" (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt), unchanged full-year guidance, and heightened investor attention on capital allocation after the company "completed share repurchases during the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Hawkins). The call emphasized TRS strength tied to data center-related project work, ongoing modular geographic expansion and services attach, and continued caution around subdued construction indicators, competitive portable storage conditions, and potential spillover risks from higher energy prices and geopolitical uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PUMA names Mark Langer CFO as Markus Neubrand steps down in 2026 transition
data.description:
• PUMA (PMMAF) has appointed Mark Langer as its new CFO, while Markus Neubrand will step down as CFO on April 30 under a mutual agreement and leave the company on September 30, 2026.
  • Langer will be responsible for finance, tax, legal, investor relations, and internal audit. Most recently, he served as CFO at Douglas AG, where he led key transformation initiatives across the finance function to drive profitable growth. Mark began his career at McKinsey & Company and Procter & Gamble. 
  • “I am convinced that with Mark, we have found a respected and highly regarded finance leader who will help PUMA deliver its financial ambitions and further reinforce investor confidence,” adds Héloïse Temple-Boyer, chair of the supervisory board.

data.title: Stellantis Non-GAAP EPS of €0.21, revenue of €38.13B; reaffirms FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Stellantis press release (STLA): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of €0.21.
  • Revenue of €38.13B (+6.5% Y/Y).
  • Adjusted operating income reached €1.0 billion, with AOI margin of 2.5% and most regions positive.
  • Industrial free cash flows were negative €1.9 billion.
  • Industrial available liquidity ended at €44.1 billion, representing 28% of trailing 12-month Net revenues and remaining within the Company's targeted 25-30% liquidity range.
  • 2026 Financial Guidance Confirmed. Company expects to improve Net revenues, AOI margin and Industrial free cash flows in 2026.

data.title: Teladoc outlines BetterHelp insurance exit run rate of $125M+ in 2026 as Integrated Care mix shift nears 70% visit-based
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Teladoc Health (TDOC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I'm pleased with our performance for the quarter with consolidated revenue and adjusted EBITDA both exceeding the midpoint of our guidance ranges" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Charles Divita), as he described Integrated Care execution and "progress we're making in scaling insurance of BetterHelp."
  • "Client preferences moving from subscription-based access towards visit-based arrangements" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita) remained central to the model shift; he said Teladoc "significantly enhanced our flagship 24/7 care offering" and expects "a moderation in the revenue headwinds" and "to exit the year with this moving to a net tailwind."
  • "We are actively developing new products for release later this year" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita), tying planned launches to investments in "our new Pulse intelligence engine" and "enhancements to our Prism Care delivery platform," with an update promised: "I look forward to providing a further update on this product innovation initiative on our second quarter call."
  • "With mounting pressure on BetterHelp's U.S. direct-to-consumer cash pay business, we took decisive action to enter the insurance market" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita). He reported the rollout is "progressing ahead of our expectations" with "30 states in Washington, D.C." live, "over 6,000 providers" credentialed/enrolled, and "insurance contracted lives" now "over 150 million."
  • "We intend to address our 2027 convertible notes in 2 phases" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita), first using "available cash" plus "new traditional term debt" and then paying the remainder "at maturity in 2027."

Outlook

  • Second-quarter consolidated revenue guidance of $597M to $626M vs. $623,716,000 (analysts’ estimate for Q2 2026 revenue).
  • "We expect 2026 consolidated revenue for the year of $2.48 billion to $2.58 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $267 million to $306 million and free cash flow of $130 million to $170 million" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita), adding "the midpoint of each of these ranges unchanged from our prior outlook."
  • "We are narrowing our 2026 revenue guidance range" for BetterHelp to "down 6.5% to down 1.0%" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita), while raising the insurance component: "full year insurance revenue in the range of $90 million to $105 million."
  • "We now expect to exit 2026 with a run rate of $125 million or more" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita) for BetterHelp insurance, alongside Q2 insurance revenue guidance of $18M to $22M.

Financial Results

  • The company reported Q1 consolidated revenue of $614M vs. $610,723,960 (analysts’ estimate for Q1 2026 revenue).
  • "Consolidated revenue was $614 million and adjusted EBITDA was $58 million" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita), and "Net loss per share was $0.36," which he said included pretax per-share amounts for "amortization of intangible assets of $0.50," "stock-based compensation of $0.08" and "restructuring costs of $0.07 per share."
  • "Free cash flow for the quarter was a net outflow of $26 million" and Teladoc ended with "$751 million in cash and cash equivalents" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita).
  • "First quarter Integrated Care revenue was $395 million" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita) and he cited "a high single-digit increase in visit revenue" offset by "lower subscription revenues," while "U.S. Integrated Care membership finished the quarter at 101.2 million members."
  • "BetterHelp's first quarter revenue was $218 million, 9% lower" year-over-year (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita), including "$13 million in insurance-based revenue"; he also reported "Average paying users" of "361,000" and BetterHelp adjusted EBITDA of "$2 million."

Q&A

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: asked about a "return to growth" in Integrated Care and a BetterHelp "turn in growth trajectory"; CEO Divita said the subscription-to-visit shift is expected to flip by year-end: "as we exit 2026, that's going to flip" to "about 70% of the memberships in visit-based arrangements," and said BetterHelp insurance is "a major catalyst for the turnaround and growth of BetterHelp in the U.S."
  • Sarah James, Cantor Fitzgerald: pressed on ACA subsidy disenrollment assumptions; CEO Divita said, "We've reflected that in our guidance" and added, "I was surprised to see a little bit higher enrollment in the first quarter," while reiterating expected moderation.
  • Daniel Grosslight, Citi: asked what is driving BetterHelp insurance upside and whether to keep DTC; CEO Divita pointed to "higher conversion" and "higher number of sessions," and said a key constraint is ensuring "the right therapist capacity to meet the demand."
  • Charles Rhyee, TD Cowen: probed cannibalization; CEO Divita said, "net of that, we're seeing about 800 basis points of revenue lift improvement" in more mature insurance states vs. cash-only states and reiterated that "over 80% drop off" in the funnel is largely due to being asked to pay out of pocket.
  • Peter Warendorf, Barclays: asked about membership and competition plus CFO timing; CEO Divita said, "The competitive environment, we operate in very competitive markets" and added, "On the CFO search, the search is ongoing."
  • Ryan MacDonald, Needham: asked about implementation delays and utilization risk under visit-based contracts; CEO Divita said, "They're moving forward" and "we've got good line of sight" to implement in the second half.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing, repeatedly challenging the durability of growth and the BetterHelp mix shift (e.g., cannibalization and ad-spend sensitivity), with pointed follow-ups such as requests to "unpack" the "800 bps" lift and insurance economics.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks ("I'm pleased," "We are confident") and measured in Q&A, acknowledging constraints ("we've got to make sure that we've got the right therapist capacity") while reiterating rollout pace and year-end expectations.
  • Versus last quarter, management provided more concrete insurance scaling markers (states, credentialed providers, covered lives, and exit run-rate language), while analyst questioning stayed concentrated on the same two pressure points: Integrated Care growth reacceleration and BetterHelp stabilization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • BetterHelp insurance scaling disclosures moved from "20 states plus Washington, D.C." and "more than 4,500" credentialed providers (Q4 2025 call) to "30 states in Washington, D.C." and "over 6,000 providers" (Q1 2026 call), alongside contracted lives rising from "more than 120 million" to "over 150 million."
  • BetterHelp insurance guidance increased from "$75 million to $90 million" for 2026 (Q4 2025 call) to "$90 million to $105 million" (Q1 2026 call), while keeping the full-year consolidated midpoint framework described as unchanged.
  • Leadership/cadence changed as CEO Divita introduced himself as "Interim Principal Financial Officer" (Q1 2026 call) and reiterated, "On the CFO search, the search is ongoing" as analysts asked for timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Mounting pressure on BetterHelp's U.S. direct-to-consumer cash pay business" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita) remained the key segment headwind, with mitigation centered on the insurance rollout and disciplined marketing spend.
  • "We've got to make sure that we've got the right therapist capacity to meet the demand" (CEO, Interim Principal Financial Officer & Director Divita) was highlighted as an operational gating factor as insurance scales.
  • Integrated Care membership and utilization sensitivity to government-program enrollment shifts remained in focus; CEO Divita said health plans "are expecting to see some continued moderation in their enrollment" and that Teladoc has "reflected that in our guidance."

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as ahead of guidance midpoints, with the most stock-sensitive updates centered on BetterHelp’s insurance acceleration (expanded state footprint, provider credentialing, covered lives, raised 2026 insurance revenue range, and an exit run rate target of $125M+). In Integrated Care, management emphasized the subscription-to-visit transition and enhanced 24/7 care as drivers of a second-half moderation in headwinds, while maintaining a disciplined cost focus and continuing to evaluate actions to address the 2027 convertible notes in two phases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Asian equities retreat as hawkish Fed cues, energy volatility, and mixed tech earnings sour sentiment
data.description:
Asian equities traded mostly lower on Thursday as investors weighed a sharp surge in oil prices, mixed corporate earnings, and a cautious policy outlook from the Federal Reserve.

Gold prices traded around $4,550 an ounce.

WTI crude futures climbed above $108 per barrel, rallying nearly 15% so far this week.

The benchmark KOSPI edged down 0.1% to around 6,680.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 1.36% to around 59,300, while the broader Topix Index fell 1.6% to 3,710 in post-holiday trading. The Japanese yen weakened past the critical 160 per dollar threshold on Thursday, reaching its lowest level since July 2024.

Retail sales in Japan rose by 1.7% year-over-year in March 2026, rebounding from a revised 0.1% decline in February and exceeding market predictions of 0.8%. Meanwhile, industrial production fell by 0.5% month-on-month, below the 1.1% expected, marking a second consecutive decline due to external challenges.

China's (SHCOMP) rose 0.12% to 4,110, remaining at its strongest level in over a month, while the Shenzhen Composite rose 0.3% to 15,167, and the offshore yuan traded around 6.84 per dollar, halting its recent decline and on track for monthly gains as stronger-than-expected PMI data.

China’s official NBS Manufacturing PMI slightly decreased to 50.3 in April 2026 from March's 50.4, exceeding expectations of 50.1. Conversely, China’s NBS Non-Manufacturing PMI dropped to 49.4 in April from 50.1 in March, falling short of the expected 49.9.

However, the RatingDog China General Manufacturing PMI rose to 52.2, up from 50.8, marking the fastest growth since December 2020.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 1.25% to 25,947.

India (SENSEX) fell 1.40%  to 76,677, reversing the previous session’s gains as sentiment weakened amid a sharp surge in crude oil prices. The Indian rupee fell to around 95.1 per dollar, marking a fresh record low as mounting external pressures continued to strain currency markets.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.24% to 8,641 in Thursday’s morning session, marking an eighth straight decline and a three-week low.

Australia's export prices rose by 0.5% in Q1 2026, down from 3.2% in Q4 2025. Import prices increased by 0.1% in Q1 2026, despite a predicted decline.

Australia’s private sector credit increased by 0.7% month-on-month in March 2026, coming in slightly above market expectations and February’s 0.6% rise. 

In the U.S. on Wednesday, all three major indexes ended lower on Wednesday after the Federal Reserve held rates unchanged and presented a split outlook, while markets positioned ahead of hyperscaler earnings.

U.S. stock futures climbed as investors assessed quarterly results from the Magnificent Seven technology firms: Dow -0.69%; S&P 500 -0.37%; Nasdaq -0.39%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Nvidia’s NVentures invests in Swedish AI legal tech Legora at $5.6B valuation, CNBC reports
data.description:
Nvidia’s (NVDA) venture arm, NVentures, has invested in Swedish AI legal tech Legora at a $5.6B valuation, as the chip giant continues to ramp up startup funding across the globe.

Legora on Thursday exclusively told CNBC that Nvidia had backed it as part of a $50M extension of its Series D, which brought the total amount to $600M following a first close in March. The extension also saw Atlassian, Adams Street Partners and Insight participate.

Nvidia (NVDA) has been ramping up startup investments in recent years, as its looked to deepen its ties to some of the world’s most promising companies, offering technical expertise and supply chain assistance, alongside hard cash.

Legora is its first bet in the legal tech sector, according to Dealroom data. The size of its investment was not disclosed, CNBC reported.

The AI startup is building AI agents and tools to help lawyers automate and streamline workflows. The company launched an ad campaign this month featuring the actor Jude Law, using the slogan: “Law just got more attractive.”

data.title: SoftBank targets $100B IPO for AI robotics venture Roze
data.description:
Japan’s SoftBank Group (SFTBY) is planning to create a new AI and robotics company designed to automate data center construction, with an eye on a roughly $100B U.S. IPO, FT reported, citing sources.

The Japanese conglomerate plans for Roze to go public as early as this year, as founder Masayoshi Son accelerates efforts to advance the group’s AI strategy.

SoftBank (SFTBY) plans to hold an analyst day in a data center facility in Texas in July to promote the IPO, the report added. The group has yet to determine the size of the stake it will sell in Roze, though it has historically retained majority ownership in its listed companies. SoftBank, for instance, continues to hold nearly 90% of Arm Holdings (ARM) following its 2023 listing.

The valuation target and IPO timeline remain subject to change, with some SoftBank (SFTBY) executives viewing the plans as ambitious amid ongoing uncertainty linked to the Middle East conflict.

Roze is the Japanese group’s planned AI and robotics venture aiming to deploy AI-driven systems and robots to speed up construction and improve efficiency, addressing a key bottleneck in AI infrastructure as demand for computing capacity surges.

data.title: Equinix raises 2026 total revenue growth outlook to 10%-11% while shifting Hampton xScale economics into Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Equinix (EQIX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “This quarter's results reflect continued strength across the business as we capitalize on a large and growing set of opportunities,” said Adaire Fox-Martin (President, CEO & Director), adding that “demand is broad-based and durable” and that “AI continues to fuel infrastructure investments that play to our strengths.”
  • Fox-Martin said “our recurring revenue grew 10% on a normalized and constant currency basis” and called Q1 “the largest quarter of total sales activity in our history,” with “total sales activity… up more than 35% year-over-year,” alongside “reducing churn.”
  • On AI positioning, Fox-Martin said “8 of the top 10 AI model providers and 4 of the top 5 neoclouds are actively expanding with Equinix,” and that they “have placed more than 110 separate network nodes with us,” adding “approximately 60% of our largest deals in Q1 were AI related.”
  • On product expansion, Fox-Martin said “The Equinix Distributed AI Hub… gives enterprises a single private low-latency connection to the entire AI ecosystem,” and said “Equinix Fabric Intelligence… [is] built directly into our fabric interconnection platform.”
  • On M&A, Fox-Martin said Equinix signed “a joint agreement with Canada Pension Plan Investment Board to purchase atNorth,” with “approximately 800 megawatts expected to come online over the next 5 years,” and said it is “expected to be immediately accretive to AFFO per share upon closing.”
  • Olivier Leonetti (Chief Financial Officer) said “recurring revenues were $2.3 billion, up 10% year-over-year,” “total revenues were $2.4 billion, up 8% year-over-year,” and “adjusted EBITDA was $1.2 billion… resulting in a 51% adjusted EBITDA margin.” He added that “quarterly AFFO surpassed the $1 billion mark for the first time… and AFFO per share was $10.79.”

Outlook

  • Leonetti said Equinix is “raising guidance across key financial metrics,” and for Q2 expects “MRR growth of 10% to 11% year-over-year.”
  • On the Hampton xScale lease timing, Leonetti said “we have adjusted the expected timing,” and that “the expanded economics are now included in our guidance for Q2,” adding, “This timing shift does not impact our full year outlook because the economics were already incorporated.”
  • For full-year 2026, Leonetti said Equinix is “raising total revenue guidance by $21 million… improving expected total revenue growth range… to 10% to 11%,” “raising adjusted EBITDA guidance by $24 million,” and “raising AFFO guidance by approximately $40 million,” which “corresponds to… AFFO per share growth range to 9% to 11%.”
  • On investment pace, Leonetti said “excluding xScale and land acquisitions, we now expect total capital expenditures to approximate the top end of our prior range at $4.1 billion,” and added the team is “evaluate[ing] opportunities to accelerate our capacity.”

Financial Results

  • Leonetti said “churn came at 1.7%,” and said Equinix is “tracking towards the low end of our 2% to 2.5% guidance range,” while “MRR per cabinet increased to $2,524.”
  • He said Equinix “increased physical and virtual net interconnections by 5,800,” “added 4,100 net cabinets billing,” and that “our backlog of cabinets sold but not yet installed is at a record level.”
  • On interconnection monetization, Fox-Martin said “total interconnection revenue was up 9% year-over-year,” “fabric revenue growth” was “26%,” and “fabric bookings were up 70% year-over-year.”
  • On capital structure and funding, Leonetti said Equinix had “$3.1 billion of cash and short-term investments,” “net leverage was 3.8x,” and said it “issued $1.5 billion of senior notes at a blended effective rate of 3.1%.”
  • On spending and returns, Leonetti said Q1 “total capital expenditures… were about $1.3 billion” and “we continue to expect mid 20% unlevered cash-on-cash returns on investment.”
  • On energy exposure, Leonetti said Equinix is “more than 90% hedged for 2026” and expects “minimum impact for 2026, even if energy prices were to remain elevated.”

Q&A

  • Michael Rollins, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked whether the “110” AI ecosystem nodes were incremental and what demand looks like; President Fox-Martin replied the nodes are “in addition to all of the nodes… being deployed by the hyperscalers,” and described demand for “connectivity to the CSPs and the NSPs,” “AI inference nodes for densely populated metros,” and “fabric access to the enterprise customer base.”
  • Cameron McVeigh, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: asked about “$140 million in pre-leasing activity” and terms; President Fox-Martin said “pricing remains firm” and presales provides “security… in terms of the infrastructure that they're defining.”
  • Matthew Niknam, Truist Securities, Inc., Research Division: asked if energy and IT cost inflation is changing behavior; President Fox-Martin said “we're not certainly seeing any pullback from customers” and reiterated “a very durable and broad-based demand environment.”
  • Frank Louthan, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked if AI inferencing changes incremental capex and returns; President Fox-Martin said “we don't see any difference in the capital,” SVP Phillip Konieczny said “the densities… are much higher,” and Konieczny added returns remain “in that mid-20s percent.”
  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho Securities USA LLC, Research Division: asked about sequential bookings seasonality and interconnection; President Fox-Martin said Q1 is “seasonally… lower” but was “our largest Q1 ever,” and said interconnection growth is supported by solutions “in pre-preview with a number of customers.”
  • Jonathan Atkin, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: asked if interconnection can outgrow overall revenue; President Fox-Martin said there is “potential for upside here, but that is not yet factored into our plans.”
  • Jyhhaw Liu, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: asked about Middle East operations; President Fox-Martin said facilities are “fully operational,” the region is “about 1% of total revenues,” and DX3’s “RFS date… be impacted due to the conflict.”
  • Nicholas Del Deo, MoffettNathanson LLC: asked CFO capital allocation philosophy; CFO Leonetti said “we want first to use debt,” and “We will use equity on an opportunistic basis.”
  • Richard Choe, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked if 1.7% churn reverses; President Fox-Martin said “we don't want to call victory too early” and “keep our churn in the range of 2% to 2.5% for the rest of the year.”
  • David Guarino, Green Street Advisors, LLC, Research Division: asked about xScale one-time fees and Manuka; President Fox-Martin said Manuka is “not timing that we have put into the short term,” and CFO Leonetti said “the rest of the xScale deals are relatively small in nature.”
  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked about cloud repatriation and edge decisioning; President Fox-Martin said it is “a hybrid multi-cloud environment” and “I wouldn't say that this is a broad-based conversation.”
  • Madison Rezaei, Bernstein Institutional Services LLC, Research Division: asked whether $4.1 billion capex is the new run-rate and return durability; President Fox-Martin said Equinix has “3 gigawatts currently either in land under control or in development,” and CFO Leonetti said “The mid 25% is a target. That's not an aspiration.”
  • Erik Rasmussen, Stifel, Nicolaus & Company, Incorporated, Research Division: asked about liquid cooling; President Fox-Martin said “36 deployments” are active, with “7 orders within Q1… up 50% Q-on-Q.”
  • Joseph Osha, Guggenheim Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about edge constraints; President Fox-Martin said “the availability of power would be the largest constraint.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with recurring diligence on AI monetization, presales commitments, churn sustainability, geopolitics, and xScale fee cadence, including questions like “how tenant appetite is changing” (Morgan Stanley) and whether churn stays low given “some of it is delayed” (JPMorgan).
  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and largely steady in Q&A, with repeated “pricing remains firm” (President Fox-Martin) and confidence statements like “we are raising guidance across key financial metrics” (CFO Leonetti), while using caution on select topics: “we don't want to call victory too early” (President Fox-Martin) and “not timing that we have put into the short term” (President Fox-Martin) on Manuka.
  • Versus last quarter, management maintained a similarly upbeat stance on AI demand; the main tonal change was more specificity on AI ecosystem buildout (nodes/neoclouds/model providers) and more explicit commentary on constraints (“availability of power”) and regional disruption (DX3 RFS impact).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership and messaging shifted with CFO succession: Q4 included “Keith's retirement announcement” (President Fox-Martin), while Q1 introduced “our new CFO, Olivier Leonetti… [who] joined us in March” (President Fox-Martin).
  • Hampton xScale timing evolved: Q4 positioned the lease as “expected in Q1” (CFO Keith Taylor), while Q1 said “our Q1 results do not include the xScale Hampton lease” and the “expanded economics are now included in our guidance for Q2” (CFO Leonetti).
  • Guidance language strengthened: Q4 expected 2026 total revenues to grow “between 9% and 10%” (CFO Taylor), while Q1 raised expected total revenue growth to “10% to 11%” (CFO Leonetti) and lifted EBITDA and AFFO guidance amounts.
  • AI narrative progressed from Q4’s “piloting AI… implement AI across their operations” framing toward Q1’s “enterprise-wide adoption at scale” and “Agentic AI… into distributed deployments” (President Fox-Martin).

Risks and Concerns

  • On energy and pricing volatility, CFO Leonetti said Equinix is “more than 90% hedged for 2026” and expects “minimum impact for 2026.”
  • On geopolitical disruption, President Fox-Martin said DX3’s “RFS date… be impacted due to the conflict,” while emphasizing “our facilities are fully operational.”
  • On capacity constraints, President Fox-Martin said “the availability of power would be the largest constraint,” describing how Equinix may “put some space on hold” to protect broader service obligations.
  • On xScale variability and forecasting, management emphasized deal complexity: “These transactions are always very complex and multifaceted” (President Fox-Martin).

Final Takeaway

Equinix management portrayed Q1 as a record sales-activity quarter with double-digit recurring revenue growth and expanding profitability, while highlighting AI inferencing and “Agentic AI” as drivers of ecosystem demand, Fabric growth, and higher-density deployments. Management raised full-year guidance ranges for total revenue growth, adjusted EBITDA, and AFFO, while emphasizing that Hampton xScale economics shifted into Q2 without changing full-year expectations. The call also surfaced practical constraints—especially power availability—and pointed to continued capacity expansion, hedged energy exposure, and an atNorth acquisition intended to extend long-term development runway in the Nordics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amazon discusses The Apprentice reboot, with Don Jr. as a potential host - WSJ
data.description:
• Amazon (AMZN) is said to be discussing a potential reboot of “The Apprentice,” the reality TV show that once starred Donald Trump and propelled him to national fame. 
  • People familiar with the matter told The Wall Street Journal, that executives at Amazon internally have discussed casting someone very close to President Trump as the host if they were to move ahead with filming new episodes: his oldest son Donald Trump Jr. 
  • “Since our acquisition of MGM, we have had preliminary internal discussions about what’s next for ‘The Apprentice’ as a property,” a spokesperson told the WSJ, but left it at that. Insiders told Variety that the show is currently “not in development” and there have not been “any discussions with anyone outside the company” about producing “The Apprentice” reboot.
  • Talks surrounding the project are still in the early stages, and Amazon executives have not yet approached the Trump family about the project, The WSJ reported.

data.title: Australia’s Q1 export prices slow to 0.5% growth as iron ore weakness weighs; iImport prices increased by 0.1%
